questions.stem: What is the primary regulatory pathway in the United States for AI/ML-based Software as a Medical Device (SaMD)?

options.A: ISO 27001 information security certification
options.B: FDA 510(k) clearance, De Novo classification, or PMA (Premarket Approval) depending on risk level, with additional AI/ML-specific guidance under the FDA's action plan for AI/ML-based SaMD
options.C: CE marking under the EU Medical Device Regulation only
options.D: IRB approval is sufficient for commercial deployment

questions.explanation: FDA regulates AI/ML medical software as SaMD. Risk-stratified pathways: 510(k) for low-to-moderate risk (substantial equivalence to predicate); De Novo for novel low-risk; PMA for high-risk (Class III). FDA's 2021 AI/ML Action Plan introduced the Predetermined Change Control Plan (PCCP) for adaptive AI systems.
questions.source: FDA. Artificial Intelligence/Machine Learning-Based Software as a Medical Device Action Plan. 2021. https://www.fda.gov/medical-devices/software-medical-device-samd/artificial-intelligence-and-machine-learning-aiml-enabled-medical-devices

questions.stem: The IDx-DR system (2018) was significant in AI medical history because it was:

options.A: The first AI system to perform robotic surgery autonomously
options.B: The first FDA-authorized AI diagnostic device to provide a screening decision without requiring clinician interpretation — detecting diabetic retinopathy from retinal images
options.C: The first AI system approved for drug discovery
options.D: The first EHR system with integrated AI decision support

questions.explanation: IDx-DR (Digital Diagnostics) received FDA De Novo authorization in April 2018 — the first AI device authorised to make a clinical decision (refer/no-refer for diabetic retinopathy) without a clinician reviewing the output. Validated on 900 patients across 10 primary care sites; sensitivity 87.2%, specificity 90.7%.
questions.source: FDA. De Novo Authorization: IDx-DR. 2018. https://www.accessdata.fda.gov/cdrh_docs/reviews/DEN180001.pdf; Abràmoff et al. NPJ Digital Medicine, 2018.

questions.stem: Why is dataset shift a critical challenge when deploying AI diagnostic models across different hospitals?

options.A: Different hospitals use different computer operating systems
options.B: AI models trained on data from one institution may perform significantly worse at another due to differences in imaging equipment, patient demographics, clinical workflows, and labelling practices — causing model predictions to degrade on out-of-distribution data
options.C: Dataset shift only affects text-based AI, not medical imaging
options.D: Hospitals share standardised data, making dataset shift unlikely

questions.explanation: Dataset shift: the joint distribution P(X,Y) differs between training and deployment. In medical AI: CT scanner model/brand affects pixel distributions; population demographics differ by region; radiologist labelling styles vary. Zech et al. (2018) showed a pneumonia CNN achieved AUC=0.931 at its training hospital but AUC=0.792–0.887 at external sites — a clinically significant gap.
questions.source: Zech et al. Variable generalization performance of a deep learning model to detect pneumonia in chest radiographs. PLOS Medicine, 2018. https://journals.plos.org/plosmedicine/article?id=10.1371/journal.pmed.1002686

questions.stem: How does AI-assisted diagnosis differ from autonomous AI diagnosis in terms of clinical workflow and regulatory requirements?

options.A: They are identical — both require no clinician involvement
options.B: AI-assisted: AI provides decision support (flags, highlights, risk scores) reviewed by a clinician who makes the final decision — lower regulatory threshold, preserves physician liability. Autonomous: AI makes the clinical decision without clinician review — higher regulatory burden (FDA PMA/De Novo), requires higher evidence bar
options.C: Autonomous AI is less regulated than AI-assisted
options.D: AI-assisted diagnosis is only allowed for low-stakes conditions

questions.explanation: Most deployed AI diagnostics are assistive — the clinician retains decision authority and liability. Autonomous systems (like IDx-DR) require stronger regulatory evidence and raise questions about liability when errors occur. The EU AI Act classifies both as high-risk; FDA SaMD framework uses intended use and risk level to determine the appropriate pathway.
questions.source: FDA. SaMD Action Plan. 2021; Topol. High-performance medicine: the convergence of human and artificial intelligence. Nature Medicine, 2019.

questions.stem: A deep learning model for pneumonia detection achieves AUC=0.96 on the internal test set but AUC=0.78 on external validation across three hospitals. The development team proposes deploying the internal model with a disclaimer. Evaluate this decision.

options.A: AUC=0.78 is acceptable — deploy immediately
options.B: The 18-point AUC gap indicates significant dataset shift and likely poor generalisation. Deployment with a disclaimer is clinically and ethically insufficient. Required steps: (1) investigate root causes (scanner type, demographic differences); (2) collect and retrain on multi-site data; (3) prospective clinical validation at target sites; (4) FDA 510(k)/De Novo submission before deployment
options.C: Deploy and monitor post-market — regulatory approval is not needed for AI software
options.D: Increase the decision threshold to compensate for lower AUC

questions.explanation: AUC=0.78 at external sites means ~22% of the ranking discriminability is lost — at clinical decision thresholds, this translates to unacceptable false negative rates for missed pneumonia. A disclaimer does not mitigate patient harm. FDA requires external validation data in 510(k)/De Novo submissions. Responsible deployment requires multi-site prospective validation.
questions.source: FDA. SaMD Action Plan. 2021; Zech et al. PLOS Medicine, 2018.

mini_quizzes.title: MQ-2 · AI in Drug Discovery and Genomics

questions.stem: What problem did AlphaFold2 (DeepMind, 2021) solve that had challenged biochemistry for over 50 years?

options.A: Synthesising novel protein sequences from scratch
options.B: Predicting a protein's 3D structure from its amino acid sequence — the protein folding problem — achieving near-experimental accuracy (median GDT_TS > 90) across most protein families
options.C: Designing personalised cancer vaccines
options.D: Sequencing the human genome at lower cost

questions.explanation: The protein folding problem: given the 1D amino acid sequence, predict the 3D tertiary structure. AlphaFold2 (Jumper et al., Nature 2021) used an Evoformer attention network and structure module to achieve a median GDT_TS score of 92.4 at CASP14 — surpassing all previous methods. DeepMind released predictions for 200M+ proteins in the AlphaFold Protein Structure Database.
questions.source: Jumper et al. Highly accurate protein structure prediction with AlphaFold. Nature, 2021. https://www.nature.com/articles/s41586-021-03819-2

questions.stem: What type of AI model architecture underpins most modern genomic sequence analysis tools for tasks like variant calling and gene expression prediction?

options.A: Classical rule-based expert systems
options.B: Deep learning models — particularly CNNs (for local sequence motifs) and Transformers (for long-range genomic dependencies) — trained on large genomic datasets
options.C: Linear regression models trained on demographic data
options.D: Decision trees applied to SNP arrays

questions.explanation: Genomic deep learning: CNNs (DeepBind, Basset) detect transcription factor binding motifs in short DNA sequences. Transformers (Enformer, Nucleotide Transformer) model long-range enhancer-promoter interactions. For variant calling, Google's DeepVariant uses a CNN on read pileup images to achieve state-of-the-art accuracy on Illumina sequencing data.
questions.source: Avsec et al. Effective gene expression prediction from sequence by integrating long-range interactions (Enformer). Nature Methods, 2021; Poplin et al. DeepVariant. Nature Biotechnology, 2018.

questions.stem: How do generative AI models accelerate de novo drug candidate design compared to traditional high-throughput screening?

options.A: Generative AI is slower — traditional screening remains the only viable approach
options.B: Traditional HTS physically screens millions of compounds at high cost and time. Generative models (VAEs, graph neural networks) learn the chemical space distribution and generate novel molecular structures with desired properties in silico — drastically reducing the candidate search space before physical synthesis
options.C: Generative AI replaces clinical trials
options.D: Generative models only work for antibody design, not small molecules

questions.explanation: Traditional HTS: synthesise and test millions of compounds → ~$1B, 10-15 years to market. Generative AI (e.g., Insilico Medicine's Chemistry42, Recursion Pharmaceuticals): sample novel molecules from learned chemical space distributions, filter by predicted ADMET properties, docking scores, and synthesisability before lab synthesis. Insilico's INS018_055 reached Phase II trials in 30 months — a record.
questions.source: Insilico Medicine. Generative AI for drug discovery. Nature Biotechnology, 2023; Stokes et al. A Deep Learning Approach to Antibiotic Discovery. Cell, 2020.

questions.stem: What is federated learning and why is it particularly valuable for training AI models on healthcare data?

options.A: Federated learning pools all patient data from multiple hospitals into a central server for training
options.B: Federated learning trains models locally at each site — only model weight updates (gradients) are shared centrally, not patient data — enabling multi-institution collaboration without violating HIPAA/GDPR patient privacy requirements
options.C: Federated learning is a data augmentation technique for medical images
options.D: Federated learning requires all hospitals to use identical imaging equipment

questions.explanation: Federated learning (McMahan et al., Google, 2017): each hospital trains locally on its data, sends only model gradients to a central aggregator (FedAvg), receives the updated global model. Patient data never leaves the institution — preserving HIPAA/GDPR compliance. HealthChain (AP-HP, 2019) demonstrated federated chest X-ray classification across 5 hospitals without data sharing.
questions.source: McMahan et al. Communication-Efficient Learning of Deep Networks from Decentralized Data. AISTATS, 2017; Rieke et al. The future of digital health with federated learning. NPJ Digital Medicine, 2020.

questions.stem: A pharmaceutical company wants to train a cancer mutation classifier using genomic data from 8 hospitals across 4 countries. GDPR and HIPAA prohibit sharing patient data across borders. Which architecture should they use and what are its limitations?

options.A: Centralise all data in a US server — GDPR does not apply to genomic data
options.B: Federated learning: train locally at each of the 8 sites, aggregate gradients centrally (FedAvg). Limitations: (1) gradient leakage attacks can partially reconstruct training data; (2) data heterogeneity across sites (non-IID) may slow convergence; (3) communication overhead across 8 sites; mitigate with differential privacy noise addition to gradients
options.C: Use transfer learning from a public genomic dataset — no multi-site collaboration needed
options.D: Only use data from one country to avoid cross-border regulations

questions.explanation: Federated learning is the standard solution for multi-jurisdiction healthcare AI. Known limitations: (1) gradient inversion attacks (Geiping et al., 2020) can reconstruct training images from gradients — mitigate with differential privacy (ε-DP noise); (2) non-IID data: genomic variant frequencies differ by ancestry/geography — FedProx or personalised FL variants help; (3) regulatory: even gradient sharing may require Data Processing Agreements under GDPR Article 28.
questions.source: McMahan et al. AISTATS, 2017; Geiping et al. Inverting Gradients. NeurIPS, 2020; Rieke et al. NPJ Digital Medicine, 2020.

mini_quizzes.title: MQ-3 · AI in Clinical Decision Support

questions.stem: What is a Clinical Decision Support System (CDSS)?

options.A: A system that replaces physician decision-making with automated diagnoses
options.B: A health information technology system that provides clinicians with patient-specific assessments, recommendations, or alerts to assist clinical decision-making at the point of care
options.C: An electronic health record database system
options.D: A system that calculates hospital billing codes automatically

questions.explanation: CDSS: provides actionable, patient-specific decision support at the point of care — drug-drug interaction alerts, sepsis early warning scores, diagnostic differentials. Ranges from simple rule-based alerts (medication dosing) to ML-based predictive models (30-day readmission risk). Distinguished from pure documentation systems (EHR).
questions.source: Shortliffe & Sepúlveda. Clinical Decision Support in the Era of Artificial Intelligence. JAMA, 2018; Berner. Clinical Decision Support Systems. Springer, 2007.

questions.stem: What is 'alert fatigue' in the context of clinical decision support systems?

options.A: Patient anxiety caused by medical alarm sounds
options.B: Clinician desensitisation to CDSS alerts due to high volume of low-specificity warnings — leading to routine overriding of alerts including clinically important ones
options.C: Technical failure of CDSS alert delivery systems
options.D: Fatigue in AI models caused by processing too many patient records

questions.explanation: Alert fatigue is a major patient safety concern: physicians override 49–96% of drug alerts in EHR systems (van der Sijs et al., JAMIA 2006). When alert specificity is low (many false positives), clinicians habituate and begin ignoring all alerts — including critical drug interaction or allergy warnings. Solution: high-specificity ML models and tiered alert prioritisation.
questions.source: van der Sijs et al. Overriding of drug safety alerts in computerized physician order entry. JAMIA, 2006; Topol. Nature Medicine, 2019.

questions.stem: Why is it important that AI clinical decision support systems provide explanations alongside their recommendations?

options.A: Explanations are not needed — clinicians should trust AI outputs unconditionally
options.B: Clinicians must understand the reasoning to critically evaluate recommendations, catch model errors, maintain accountability for decisions, and comply with GDPR Article 22 and FDA guidance requiring meaningful explanations for automated clinical decisions
options.C: Explanations are only required for billing purposes
options.D: AI explanations are optional and provide no clinical benefit

questions.explanation: Explainability in clinical AI: (1) safety — clinicians can detect when model reasoning is spurious; (2) accountability — physician retains decision responsibility; (3) regulatory — GDPR Art. 22 right to explanation; FDA guidance recommends transparency for SaMD. SHAP/LIME provide feature-level explanations; attention visualisation shows image regions driving predictions.
questions.source: Shortliffe & Sepúlveda. JAMA, 2018; GDPR Article 22; FDA SaMD Action Plan. 2021.

questions.stem: How does an early warning score (EWS) based on simple vital sign thresholds differ from an ML-based sepsis prediction model in performance and interpretability?

options.A: Both have identical sensitivity, specificity, and interpretability
options.B: Rule-based EWS (e.g., NEWS2): transparent, interpretable, consistent; typically lower sensitivity/specificity (AUROC ~0.70–0.75 for sepsis). ML models (e.g., Epic Sepsis Model, InSight): higher AUROC (~0.74–0.85) by capturing non-linear interactions between features, but less transparent and subject to institutional bias
options.C: ML models always outperform EWS by >20 AUROC points
options.D: EWS is more accurate than ML models for all patient populations

questions.explanation: NEWS2 (Royal College of Physicians): 6 vital signs → integer score. Transparent, implemented in paper form. Epic Sepsis Model: 40+ EHR features → gradient boosted tree (AUROC 0.76 in external validation, Wong et al. 2021). The Epic model generated high alert volume with 18% sensitivity for actual sepsis at one validation — illustrating that higher AUROC does not always translate to better clinical utility.
questions.source: Wong et al. External validation of a widely implemented proprietary sepsis prediction model. JAMA Internal Medicine, 2021; Royal College of Physicians. NEWS2. 2017.

questions.stem: You are designing an ML-based 24-hour ICU mortality prediction system. Which design choices address the key clinical AI deployment requirements?

options.A: Maximise AUC only — no other requirements matter
options.B: (1) Train on multi-site ICU data (MIMIC-IV + eICU) for generalisability; (2) use SHAP values to explain each prediction to clinicians; (3) calibrate model probabilities (Platt scaling) so P=0.8 truly means 80% risk; (4) set threshold using clinical cost-benefit analysis (FN cost >> FP cost for ICU mortality); (5) prospective clinical trial before deployment
options.C: Deploy immediately after achieving AUC>0.85 on a single-site test set
options.D: Use a black-box deep learning model with no explanations to maximise AUC

questions.explanation: ICU mortality prediction requirements: (1) Generalisation: MIMIC-IV (n=76K) + eICU (n=200K) provide diverse populations; (2) Explainability: SHAP top features (lactate, age, SOFA) per patient; (3) Calibration: well-calibrated probabilities enable risk communication; (4) Threshold: ICU FN >> FP cost → high recall threshold; (5) Prospective validation: randomised controlled trial for clinical impact evidence (e.g., MIMIC-CDSS RCT design).
questions.source: Johnson et al. MIMIC-IV. Scientific Data, 2023; Harutyunyan et al. Multitask learning for EHR. Scientific Data, 2019.

mini_quizzes.title: MQ-4 · AI Ethics and Governance in Healthcare

questions.stem: What does 'informed consent' require when a patient's data is used to train an AI diagnostic model?

options.A: No consent is needed — anonymised data is freely usable
options.B: Patients must be informed about how their data will be used (including AI training), the risks involved, and must provide voluntary, specific consent — or a lawful basis under GDPR (Article 9 for health data) and HIPAA must be established
options.C: Only the hospital CEO's signature is required
options.D: Consent is only needed for data from patients over 65

questions.explanation: GDPR Article 9 classifies health data as special category — requires explicit consent or another lawful basis (e.g., public interest, Article 9(2)(h)/(j)). HIPAA requires a signed authorisation for non-treatment uses. De-identification (Safe Harbor or Expert Determination) can enable use without consent but does not eliminate re-identification risk for genomic data.
questions.source: GDPR Articles 6 & 9. https://gdpr-info.eu/art-9-gdpr; HHS HIPAA Privacy Rule. 45 CFR §164.514.

questions.stem: The Obermeyer et al. (2019) study found that an algorithm used by US health systems to identify high-risk patients had racial bias. What was the root cause?

options.A: The algorithm intentionally discriminated by including race as a feature
options.B: The algorithm used healthcare cost as a proxy for medical need; due to systemic inequities, Black patients historically incurred lower costs than equally ill white patients, causing them to be assigned lower risk scores
options.C: The training dataset only included white patients
options.D: The algorithm was trained on European health data

questions.explanation: Proxy discrimination: healthcare cost ≈ medical need (the proxy) was racially confounded — equal illness severity generated different costs due to structural barriers to care for Black patients. Replacing cost with illness severity directly reduced the measured racial disparity by 84%. Illustrates how bias can arise without explicitly including protected attributes.
questions.source: Obermeyer et al. Dissecting racial bias in an algorithm used to manage the health of populations. Science, 2019. https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/31649194

questions.stem: Why must healthcare AI systems undergo prospective clinical validation rather than relying solely on retrospective test-set performance?

options.A: Retrospective performance is always sufficient for clinical deployment
options.B: Retrospective metrics measure performance on historical data under controlled conditions; prospective trials measure real-world clinical impact — accounting for alert fatigue, workflow integration, clinician behaviour changes, and temporal distribution shift that retrospective studies cannot capture
options.C: Prospective validation is only required for surgical AI
options.D: Retrospective studies are more rigorous than prospective trials

questions.explanation: Key gap: a model may achieve AUC=0.85 retrospectively but fail to improve outcomes prospectively if clinicians ignore alerts, if workflow integration is poor, or if the model is deployed on a shifted patient population. Prospective RCTs (e.g., Sendak et al. on sepsis AI) are the gold standard for demonstrating clinical benefit and safety.
questions.source: Topol. Nature Medicine, 2019; Sendak et al. Real-World Integration of a Sepsis DL Technology into Clinical Practice. NEJM Catalyst, 2020.

questions.stem: How do HIPAA's de-identification standards differ in their approach, and what residual risk does each carry?

options.A: HIPAA has only one de-identification standard
options.B: Safe Harbor: remove 18 specified identifiers (name, DOB, ZIP, etc.) — simple but does not account for re-identification via combinations of quasi-identifiers. Expert Determination: statistical expert certifies re-identification risk is very small — more rigorous but still imperfect, especially for rare conditions or genomic data
options.C: Both standards completely eliminate re-identification risk
options.D: Expert Determination is less rigorous than Safe Harbor

questions.explanation: Safe Harbor (45 CFR §164.514(b)): remove 18 identifiers. Risk: quasi-identifier combinations (ZIP + DOB + sex) can uniquely identify 87% of the US population (Sweeney, 2000). Expert Determination (§164.514(b)(1)): statistical analysis of re-identification risk — more thorough. Genomic data is never truly de-identifiable — a genome is inherently a unique identifier.
questions.source: HHS HIPAA Privacy Rule. 45 CFR §164.514; Sweeney. Simple demographics often identify people uniquely. Health Security, 2000.

questions.stem: An AI triage system deployed in an emergency department shows significantly lower accuracy for elderly patients (>75 years) than for middle-aged adults, despite good overall performance. The vendor argues overall accuracy is within spec. Evaluate this argument and propose a governance response.

options.A: The vendor is correct — overall accuracy is the only relevant metric
options.B: The vendor's argument is ethically and legally insufficient. Subgroup performance disparities violate equity principles and may constitute age discrimination under the Equality Act/ADA. Governance response: (1) mandate disaggregated performance reporting by age, sex, and comorbidity at procurement; (2) require retraining or recalibration for the elderly subgroup; (3) implement human override requirements for patients >75; (4) report to FDA/competent authority as a device performance issue
options.C: Accept the system but restrict deployment to patients under 75
options.D: No action needed until a patient harm event occurs

questions.explanation: Subgroup disparities in life-critical triage are patient safety issues, not merely statistical artefacts. EU AI Act Art. 9 requires risk management for high-risk AI. FDA post-market surveillance requirements mandate reporting of performance degradation. Procurement contracts should specify disaggregated performance thresholds. The ADA and Equality Act prohibit age-based discrimination in healthcare services, including AI-mediated ones.
questions.source: EU AI Act Articles 9 & 15. 2024; FDA. Postmarket Management of Cybersecurity in Medical Devices. 2023; Obermeyer et al. Science, 2019.
